content:
# Romans Chapter 11 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-05

This log records all verses in Romans Chapter 11 where the three translators (EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) significantly diverged in their renderings, along with SOPHIA's resolution and reasoning.

---

## 11:1 — "has God rejected his people?" vs. "has God rejected his own people, the nation of Israel?"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "I say then, did the God reject the people of him? May it never be! For I also am an Israelite, from [the] seed of Abraham, of [the] tribe of Benjamin." | Literal; "May it never be" for μὴ γένοιτο |
| LOGOS | "I ask, then, has God rejected his people? By no means! For I myself am an Israelite, a descendant of Abraham, a member of the tribe of Benjamin." | Smooth English; "By no means" |
| PNEUMA | "I ask, then, has God rejected his own people, the nation of Israel? Of course not! I myself am an Israelite, a descendant of Abraham and a member of the tribe of Benjamin." | Adds "his own," "the nation of Israel"; "Of course not" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. PNEUMA's "the nation of Israel" is an explanatory addition not in the Greek. "By no means" captures μὴ γένοιτο more forcefully than PNEUMA's colloquial "Of course not."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 11:2 — "foreknew" vs. "chose from the very beginning"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "The God did not reject the people of him whom he foreknew." | "Foreknew" (προέγνω) — literal |
| LOGOS | "God has not rejected his people whom he foreknew." | Same verb, smoother syntax |
| PNEUMA | "No, God has not rejected his own people, whom he chose from the very beginning." | Interprets "foreknew" as "chose from the very beginning" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Foreknew" (προέγνω) carries a specific theological meaning in Paul — not mere prior knowledge but relational, elective knowing. PNEUMA's "chose from the very beginning" captures part of this but loses the "knowing" dimension and adds "from the very beginning."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. "Foreknew" retained.

---

## 11:4 — "God's reply" vs. "divine response" vs. "God's reply? He said"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "But what does the divine response say to him? 'I have reserved for myself seven thousand men who did not bend [the] knee to the Baal.'" | "Divine response" (χρηματισμός); "reserved"; "the Baal" with article |
| LOGOS | "But what is God's reply to him? 'I have kept for myself seven thousand men who have not bowed the knee to Baal.'" | "God's reply"; "kept" |
| PNEUMA | "And do you remember God's reply? He said, 'No, I have 7,000 others who have never bowed down to Baal!'" | "Do you remember" added; numeral "7,000"; "others" added; exclamation mark |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. EMET's "divine response" is a precise translation of χρηματισμός but sounds stilted. PNEUMA adds "Do you remember" and "others" not in the text, and uses a numeral instead of spelling out the number.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 11:5 — "remnant, chosen by grace" vs. "a few... have remained faithful because of God's grace"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "So then also in the present time a remnant according to [the] election of grace has come to be." | "Remnant according to election of grace" — literal genitive chain |
| LOGOS | "So too at the present time there is a remnant, chosen by grace." | Concise; "chosen by grace" |
| PNEUMA | "It is the same today, for a few of the people of Israel have remained faithful because of God's grace — his undeserved kindness in choosing them." | "A few" for remnant; adds "undeserved kindness" as gloss |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. PNEUMA replaces the theologically loaded term "remnant" (λεῖμμα) with "a few," losing the significant OT remnant theology. PNEUMA also adds "undeserved kindness" as an explanatory gloss on grace.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. "Remnant" preserved as a theological term.

---

## 11:7 — "The elect obtained it, but the rest were hardened"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the election obtained [it], and the rest were hardened" | "The election" (ἡ ἐκλογή) — abstract noun |
| LOGOS | "The elect obtained it, but the rest were hardened" | "The elect" — renders abstract as concrete |
| PNEUMA | "A few have — the ones God has chosen — but the hearts of the rest were hardened." | Adds "the hearts of"; parenthetical explanation |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. EMET's "the election" preserves the abstract noun but is unclear in English. LOGOS's "the elect" correctly interprets the metonymy. PNEUMA adds "the hearts of" (not in Greek — the text says simply "were hardened," ἐπωρώθησαν).

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 11:11 — "did they stumble in order to fall?" vs. "stumble and fall beyond recovery?"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "did they stumble that they might fall? May it never be!" | Purpose clause preserved |
| LOGOS | "did they stumble in order to fall? By no means!" | Same structure, formal English |
| PNEUMA | "Did God's people stumble and fall beyond recovery? Of course not!" | "Fall beyond recovery" — interpretive; adds "God's people" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. Paul's rhetorical question uses a purpose clause (ἵνα πέσωσιν — "in order that they might fall"), asking whether the stumbling had falling as its purpose. PNEUMA changes this to a result question ("fall beyond recovery"), which is a valid interpretation but alters the Greek syntax.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 11:12 — "their full inclusion" vs. "the fullness of them"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "how much more the fullness of them?" | "Fullness" (πλήρωμα) — literal |
| LOGOS | "how much more will their full inclusion mean!" | "Full inclusion" — interprets πλήρωμα |
| PNEUMA | "think how much greater a blessing the world will share when they finally accept it." | Full paraphrase; "when they finally accept it" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "full inclusion" which interprets πλήρωμα in the context of the remnant/whole distinction Paul is drawing. EMET's "fullness" is more literal but ambiguous. PNEUMA's paraphrase loses the contrast structure entirely.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 11:14 — "my fellow Jews" vs. "the flesh of me"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "if somehow I might provoke to jealousy the flesh of me and save some of them" | "The flesh of me" (τὴν σάρκα μου) — hyper-literal |
| LOGOS | "in order somehow to make my fellow Jews jealous, and thus save some of them" | "Fellow Jews" — interprets σάρκα μου |
| PNEUMA | "I want somehow to make the people of Israel jealous of what you Gentiles have, so I might save some of them" | "People of Israel"; adds "of what you Gentiles have" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. EMET's "the flesh of me" is opaque in English — σάρκα μου here means "my own people" or "my kinsmen according to the flesh." LOGOS's "fellow Jews" is clear and accurate. PNEUMA adds "of what you Gentiles have" which is not in the text.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 11:15 — "life from the dead"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "what [will] the acceptance [be] except life from [the] dead?" | Elliptical; literal |
| LOGOS | "what will their acceptance mean but life from the dead?" | Complete sentence |
| PNEUMA | "their acceptance will be even more wonderful. It will be life for those who were dead!" | Splits into two sentences; "for those who were dead" interprets the phrase |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. The phrase ζωὴ ἐκ νεκρῶν ("life from the dead") is deliberately ambiguous — it could mean literal resurrection, spiritual renewal, or eschatological transformation. PNEUMA's "life for those who were dead" narrows it to one interpretation.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. The ambiguity of "life from the dead" is preserved.

---

## 11:16 — "dough offered as firstfruits" vs. "portion given as an offering"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "And if the firstfruit [is] holy, also the lump; and if the root [is] holy, also the branches." | Compact; "firstfruit/lump" |
| LOGOS | "If the dough offered as firstfruits is holy, so is the whole lump, and if the root is holy, so are the branches." | "Dough offered as firstfruits" — explanatory |
| PNEUMA | "And since Abraham and the other patriarchs were holy, their descendants will also be holy — just as the entire batch of dough is holy because the portion given as an offering is holy. For if the roots of the tree are holy, the branches will be, too." | Identifies firstfruits with Abraham; expands extensively; pluralizes "roots" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. PNEUMA's identification of the firstfruits with "Abraham and the other patriarchs" is one interpretive option but should not be embedded in the translation text. PNEUMA also pluralizes "root" to "roots," which differs from the Greek singular ῥίζα.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 11:17 — "nourishing root" vs. "root of the fatness" vs. "rich nourishment from the root"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "became a partaker of the root of the fatness of the olive tree" | "Root of the fatness" — literal genitive (τῆς ῥίζης τῆς πιότητος) |
| LOGOS | "now share in the nourishing root of the olive tree" | "Nourishing root" — attributive rendering |
| PNEUMA | "sharing in the rich nourishment from the root of God's special olive tree" | "Rich nourishment from the root"; adds "God's special" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. EMET's "root of the fatness" is awkward in English. LOGOS's "nourishing root" captures the genitive of quality. PNEUMA's "God's special" is an addition not in the Greek.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 11:22 — "kindness and severity" — key theological vocabulary

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "See therefore [the] kindness and [the] severity of God" | "See" for ἴδε; articles preserved |
| LOGOS | "Note then the kindness and the severity of God" | "Note" for ἴδε |
| PNEUMA | "Notice how God is both kind and severe." | Converts nouns to adjectives; adds "both" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. PNEUMA's conversion from abstract nouns ("kindness and severity") to adjectives ("kind and severe") loses the weight of Paul's theological statement — he is naming divine attributes, not merely describing behavior.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Abstract nouns "kindness and severity" preserved.

---

## 11:25 — "mystery" vs. "this mystery" — rendering of μυστήριον

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "I do not want you to be ignorant, brothers, of this mystery — that you may not be wise in yourselves — that a hardening from a part has come upon Israel" | "Hardening from a part" — literal; "wise in yourselves" |
| LOGOS | "I do not want you to be unaware of this mystery, brothers: a partial hardening has come upon Israel, until the fullness of the Gentiles has come in." | "Partial hardening" — renders ἀπὸ μέρους as adjective |
| PNEUMA | "Some of the people of Israel have hard hearts, but this will last only until the full number of Gentiles comes to Christ." | "Hard hearts" for hardening; "full number" for πλήρωμα; adds "comes to Christ" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. EMET's "hardening from a part" is awkward; LOGOS's "partial hardening" is clearer. PNEUMA's "hard hearts" domesticates the technical term πώρωσις and adds "comes to Christ" which is not in the text (Paul says "comes in," εἰσέλθῃ).

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 11:26 — "The Deliverer will come from Zion" vs. "from Jerusalem"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "The deliverer will come from Sion; he will turn away ungodliness from Iakob." | "Sion"; "Iakob" — transliterations |
| LOGOS | "The Deliverer will come from Zion, he will banish ungodliness from Jacob" | "Zion"; "Jacob" — anglicized; "banish" |
| PNEUMA | "The one who rescues will come from Jerusalem, and he will turn Israel away from ungodliness." | "Jerusalem" for Zion; "The one who rescues" for ὁ ῥυόμενος; "Israel" for Jacob |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. PNEUMA's substitution of "Jerusalem" for "Zion" (Σιών) loses the specific theological resonance. "Zion" carries eschatological weight that "Jerusalem" as a geographical reference does not. Similarly, "Jacob" should be preserved as it echoes the patriarchal election theme of chapter 9.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. "Zion" and "Jacob" retained.

---

## 11:29 — "irrevocable" vs. "can never be withdrawn"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "For the gifts and the calling of the God [are] irrevocable." | "Irrevocable" for ἀμεταμέλητα |
| LOGOS | "For the gifts and the calling of God are irrevocable." | Same |
| PNEUMA | "For God's gifts and his call can never be withdrawn." | "Can never be withdrawn" — paraphrastic but powerful |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** All three agree on the core meaning. Adopted LOGOS/EMET's "irrevocable" (ἀμεταμέλητα, literally "not-to-be-repented-of") as the more concise and theologically precise term. PNEUMA's "can never be withdrawn" is accurate but wordier.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS/EMET. "Irrevocable" retained.

---

## 11:32 — "consigned all to disobedience" vs. "imprisoned everyone in disobedience"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "For the God shut up all into disobedience, that he might have mercy on all." | "Shut up" for συνέκλεισεν |
| LOGOS | "For God has consigned all to disobedience, that he may have mercy on all." | "Consigned" for συνέκλεισεν |
| PNEUMA | "For God has imprisoned everyone in disobedience so he could have mercy on everyone." | "Imprisoned" for συνέκλεισεν; "everyone" for πάντας (twice) |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Consigned" captures the legal/judicial nuance of συνέκλεισεν without the physical confinement image of "imprisoned" (PNEUMA) or the informality of "shut up" (EMET). All three preserve the crucial double "all" (πάντας... πάντων).

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 11:33 — "Oh, the depth" — doxological register

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "O depth of riches and wisdom and knowledge of God! How unsearchable [are] the judgments of him, and untraceable the ways of him!" | "O" vocative; "untraceable" for ἀνεξιχνίαστοι |
| LOGOS | "Oh, the depth of the riches and wisdom and knowledge of God! How unsearchable are his judgments and how inscrutable his ways!" | "Oh" exclamatory; "inscrutable" for ἀνεξιχνίαστοι |
| PNEUMA | "Oh, how great are God's riches and wisdom and knowledge! How impossible it is for us to understand his decisions and his ways!" | "How great" — restructures; "impossible to understand" — interprets; "decisions" for κρίματα |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. The key divergence is on ἀνεξιχνίαστοι: EMET's "untraceable" is etymologically precise (cannot be traced out), LOGOS's "inscrutable" is more standard English, PNEUMA's "impossible to understand" is too explanatory. Also, PNEUMA's "decisions" weakens κρίματα ("judgments" — carrying judicial weight).

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. "Inscrutable" and "judgments" retained.

---

## 11:36 — "For from him and through him and to him are all things"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Because from him and through him and to him [are] all things. To him [be] the glory into the ages. Amen." | "Because" for ὅτι; "into the ages" |
| LOGOS | "For from him and through him and to him are all things. To him be glory forever. Amen." | "For" for ὅτι; "forever" |
| PNEUMA | "For everything comes from him and exists by his power and is intended for his glory. All glory to him forever! Amen." | Interprets each preposition; "by his power" for δι᾽ αὐτοῦ; "intended for his glory" for εἰς αὐτόν |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. The three prepositions (ἐξ, διά, εἰς) are deliberately compact and should remain so. PNEUMA's interpretation ("exists by his power," "intended for his glory") adds theological content not explicit in the Greek, and changes εἰς αὐτόν ("to him") into "for his glory," which conflates the destination with the doxology.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. The trinitarian-resonant prepositional formula preserved.

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Occurrences | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA's substitution of theological terms with explanations | vv.2, 5, 7, 22, 25, 33 | "Foreknew" → "chose"; "remnant" → "a few"; "hardening" → "hard hearts" |
| PNEUMA's geographic/name substitutions | v.26 | "Zion" → "Jerusalem"; "Jacob" → "Israel" |
| PNEUMA's interpretive additions | vv.1, 4, 14, 16, 17, 25 | "The nation of Israel," "Do you remember," "God's special," "comes to Christ" |
| EMET's transliterations vs. anglicized names | vv.1, 2, 26 | "Sion/Zion," "Iakob/Jacob," "Elias/Elijah" |
| Preserving ambiguity vs. resolving it | vv.12, 15, 16 | πλήρωμα: "fullness" vs. "full inclusion" vs. "full number" |
| Register differences in exclamatory passages | v.33 | "O depth" vs. "Oh, the depth" vs. "Oh, how great" |
| LOGOS-SOPHIA alignment | Most verses | LOGOS's formal-equivalent approach adopted in nearly every case |

## Overall Pattern Analysis

Romans 11 is the resolution of the Israel question (chs. 9-11), culminating in the great doxology of vv.33-36. The translators diverge most significantly on theological vocabulary: PNEUMA consistently replaces Paul's technical terms ("remnant," "foreknew," "hardening," "consigned," "judgments") with accessible modern equivalents, while EMET and LOGOS preserve them. The olive tree passage (vv.17-24) shows the least divergence — all three translators handle the extended metaphor similarly. The greatest divergence appears in the doxology (vv.33-36), where register and theological precision matter most. PNEUMA interprets the compact prepositional theology of v.36 into explanatory clauses, while LOGOS preserves the majestic brevity. SOPHIA consistently favored LOGOS's approach of retaining theological vocabulary and structural elegance, relying on commentary notes rather than translation text to provide explanation.

---
